title: TaskFlow server gets slower throughout the day, requires daily restart
body:
Our self-hosted TaskFlow instance starts the day running fine but gets progressively slower. By late afternoon, page loads take 10+ seconds and the API times out. We've been restarting the server every evening for the past week. Memory usage on the server climbs from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ by end of day. We have about 200 active users.

I've attached a screenshot of our Grafana dashboard showing the memory climb. The pattern is very consistent day-to-day.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the detailed report and the Grafana data — that consistent memory climb pattern is really helpful.

I'd like to understand the timeline a bit better: you mentioned this has been happening for the past week. Was there anything that changed around the time this started — a TaskFlow upgrade, a configuration change, a jump in user count, or maybe a new integration or workflow your team started using? And was the server running stable for a long time before this, or is this a relatively new deployment?

Understanding what shifted will help us narrow down where the leak is coming from.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Good question — yes, we upgraded from TaskFlow v2.2 to v2.3 about a week ago, which lines up exactly with when this started. The server had been rock solid on v2.2 for months before that. No other changes on our end — same VM, same user base, no new integrations. I did glance at the v2.3 changelog and noticed it mentioned 'improved real-time notifications' as a new feature, which stood out to me since the memory growth seems to correlate with active usage during the day. When users go home, the memory stops climbing but doesn't drop back down either.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's a really strong clue — the v2.3 upgrade timing lines up perfectly, and the real-time notifications feature is a natural suspect for a memory leak, especially with the pattern you're describing (climbs with usage, never releases).

I'd love to explore that thread a bit more. Can you tell me about how your team uses TaskFlow during the day? For instance, do people tend to keep TaskFlow open in a browser tab all day, or do they open and close it frequently? And have you noticed anything about the real-time notifications themselves — are they working as expected, or have you seen anything odd like duplicate notifications, notifications that stop arriving, or connections dropping?

I'm trying to understand whether the leak might be tied to accumulating connections that aren't being cleaned up when users navigate away or close tabs.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Most of our users keep TaskFlow open in a browser tab throughout the day — it's their main project management tool so they're constantly checking tasks, updating statuses, etc. I'd say a typical user makes maybe 20-30 requests per hour just from normal usage. As for the notifications themselves, they actually seem to work fine — people are getting their notifications as expected. I haven't heard complaints about duplicates or dropped notifications. The interesting thing is that the memory growth seems proportional to overall API activity, not just the number of connected users. Like, even if the same 200 people are connected all day, the memory keeps climbing steadily rather than plateauing once everyone's connected. That's what made me think it might be leaking something on every request rather than just on connection setup.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Memory leak in v2.3: per-request memory growth likely introduced by real-time notifications feature","problem":"After upgrading from TaskFlow v2.2 to v2.3, the server exhibits a steady memory leak — climbing from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ over a workday — causing progressive slowdown (10+ second page loads, API timeouts) and requiring daily restarts. The issue began exactly with the v2.3 upgrade after months of stability on v2.2.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The v2.3 'improved real-time notifications' feature likely introduces a per-request memory allocation (e.g., event objects, notification payloads, middleware state, or subscriber lookups) that is never released. The leak is proportional to total API request volume (~200 users × 20-30 requests/hour = ~4,000-6,000 requests/hour), not to the number of persistent connections, which rules out a WebSocket/SSE connection leak and points to something allocated in the per-request hot path — possibly notification fan-out logic, event history buffering, or a listener/callback registered on each request but never deregistered.","reproduction_steps":["Deploy TaskFlow v2.3 on a self-hosted instance (single server VM)","Connect ~200 active users (or simulate equivalent load of ~4,000-6,000 API requests/hour)","Monitor server memory via Grafana or similar over 6-8 hours","Observe steady linear memory growth from ~500MB toward 4GB+ with no plateau or release","Confirm memory stops climbing when request volume drops (e.g., after hours) but does not reclaim"],"environment":"Self-hosted TaskFlow v2.3, ~200 active users, single VM. Previously stable on v2.2 for months. Same infrastructure, user base, and integrations before and after upgrade.","severity":"high","impact":"All 200 users experience progressively degraded performance throughout the day, with API timeouts and 10+ second page loads by afternoon. Requires daily manual server restarts to maintain usability.","recommended_fix":"Diff the v2.3 real-time notifications code against v2.2, focusing on any per-request middleware or event-handling code paths. Look for: (1) event listeners or callbacks registered per request but never removed, (2) notification payloads or event objects appended to in-memory collections without eviction, (3) per-request context or state captured in closures that prevent garbage collection. A heap dump comparison between startup and after several hours of load should confirm which object types are accumulating. As an immediate mitigation, the reporter could consider rolling back to v2.2 or disabling the real-time notifications feature if a feature flag exists.","proposed_test_case":"Load test that simulates 200 concurrent users making 25 requests/hour each against a v2.3 instance over a 4-hour window, asserting that resident memory stays within a bounded range (e.g., does not exceed startup memory + 500MB). Compare the same test against v2.2 as a baseline. Additionally, a unit test for the notification middleware verifying that per-request allocations are properly released after response completion.","information_gaps":["Server runtime and language (Node.js, Java, Python, etc.) — determines which profiling tools to use for heap analysis","Whether a feature flag or configuration option exists to disable real-time notifications in v2.3, which would confirm the feature as the source","Whether the memory growth rate is strictly linear or accelerating (could indicate cascading retention)"]}
